title: Rāmāyaṇa
author: Vālmīki
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Epic Literature; dcs_subject=Ramayana; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Valmīki; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Vālmīki's Rāmāyaṇa is a foundational epic of Indian civilization, telling the story of Rāma's exile, the abduction of Sītā, and his quest to rescue her. It is the earliest known Sanskrit kāvya (ādikāvya) and has exerted immense influence on literature, religion, art, and morality across South and Southeast Asia. It is one of the most central texts in the entire Sanskrit corpus.

title: Carakasaṃhitā
author: Caraka
classification_notes: The Carakasaṃhitā is the foundational text of Ayurvedic internal medicine, traditionally attributed to Caraka. It remains a living source of medical knowledge and is universally studied in Ayurveda.

title: Mūlamadhyamakārikāḥ
author: Nāgārjuna
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Nāgārjuna; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Mūlamadhyamakārikāḥ is the magnum opus of Nāgārjuna, the foundational text of the Madhyamaka school of Buddhist philosophy. It is one of the most studied and commented-upon works in the entire Buddhist canon.

title: Yogasūtra
author: Patañjali
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Yoga; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Patañjali; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Yogasūtra of Patañjali is the foundational text of the Yoga darśana, systematizing the theory and practice of yoga in four pādas (chapters). It defines the eight-limbed path (aṣṭāṅga-yoga) and is arguably the most widely known work of Indian philosophy globally, studied across disciplines from Indology to modern yoga practice. Despite its brevity, its influence on both classical Indian thought and global spirituality is immense.

title: Bhagavadgītā
author: Vyāsa
classification_notes: The Bhagavadgītā (BhG) is a 700-verse poem within the Mahābhārata (6.23–40). Attributed to Vyāsa, it is a central Vedāntic, yogic, and dharmic dialogue between Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna. As a standalone text of 13,361 words, it is the most widely read and commented-upon philosophical poem in the Sanskrit corpus, canonical globally and within the epic tradition.
